stem:
You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 22-year-old previously well thin man with sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild breathlessness 4 hours ago. He has never smoked and has no past medical history. He has no trauma, no fever, and no preceding illness. On examination he has reduced breath sounds on the right with normal trachea midline. Vital signs are stable.

Chest X-ray confirms a moderate right primary pneumothorax with an interpleural distance of 3 cm at the hilum and no mediastinal shift. There is no underlying lung disease on imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Trachea midline, reduced right breath sounds, hyper-resonant percussion right, no surgical emphysema
data_table.Investigations: CXR: right pneumothorax interpleural distance 3 cm at hilum, no shift, no underlying disease

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Urgent video-assisted thoracoscopic pleurodesis as definitive surgical management for first presentation
options.rationale: VATS pleurodesis is reserved for recurrent pneumothorax, persistent air leak, or first occurrence in high-risk occupations. It is not first-line for a stable primary pneumothorax at first presentation. (Source: Lung Foundation Australia - Pneumothorax)

options.text: Insertion of a 28F intercostal catheter as the first procedure for any visible primary pneumothorax
options.rationale: Large-bore intercostal catheters cause more pain and longer hospital stays than aspiration or small-bore drains in primary pneumothorax, and are not first-line in a stable patient. (Source: Lung Foundation Australia - Pneumothorax)

options.text: Observation only with high-flow oxygen by mask and reassessment with serial chest X-rays at 24 hours
options.rationale: Conservative management is acceptable for small primary pneumothorax (under 2 cm) with minimal symptoms but is not first choice for a 3 cm moderately symptomatic pneumothorax. (Source: Lung Foundation Australia - Pneumothorax)

options.text: Needle aspiration of the right pleural space with a 14 to 16 gauge cannula and post-procedure chest X-ray
options.rationale: British Thoracic Society and Australian guidance support needle aspiration as first-line for a symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax with interpleural distance more than 2 cm at the hilum. Aspiration succeeds in around 60% of cases and avoids intercostal catheter insertion. (Source: Lung Foundation Australia - Pneumothorax)

options.text: Discharge home with analgesia and outpatient review in 2 weeks with repeat chest X-ray then
options.rationale: A symptomatic moderate primary pneumothorax requires active management before discharge. Home discharge without aspiration or drainage risks progression and tension physiology. (Source: Lung Foundation Australia - Pneumothorax)

explanation.summary: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax most commonly affects tall, thin, young men and is managed by size and symptoms. Interpleural distance under 2 cm at the hilum with minimal symptoms is observed. Larger or symptomatic primary pneumothoraces are treated by needle aspiration first, with small-bore intercostal catheter for failure. Tension physiology mandates immediate needle decompression. After a first primary pneumothorax, recurrence rate is approximately 30% within 1 year; VATS pleurodesis or bullectomy is offered after a second event or for persistent air leak.
key_points: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax under 2 cm: observation only
Larger or symptomatic: needle aspiration first-line
Persistent air leak or recurrence: small-bore intercostal catheter then VATS
Recurrence after first event around 30% within 1 year
explanation.pearls: Tall thin young males who smoke have a 7 to 10-fold higher risk of recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax, and smoking cessation is the single most important secondary prevention measure even after surgical pleurodesis.

stem: You are the general medical registrar admitting a 58-year-old woman with 3 weeks of low-grade fever, weight loss, and night sweats. She has a known bioprosthetic aortic valve replaced 14 months ago for severe aortic stenosis. Today she has developed a new soft early diastolic murmur on the left sternal edge. There are no embolic phenomena on examination. Three sets of blood cultures have been drawn from separate sites and a transthoracic echocardiogram has been ordered. There are no penicillin allergies. The team plans empirical antibiotics while awaiting culture and echocardiographic results.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 124/68 mmHg, Temp 38.4 degrees C, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: New aortic regurgitation murmur, no Janeway lesions, no Osler nodes, no splinter haemorrhages
data_table.Investigations: CRP 88 mg/L, WCC 12 x10^9/L, Hb 108 g/L (normocytic), creatinine 84 micromol/L, ECG sinus rhythm with no new conduction delay

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy as broad-spectrum cover for streptococci and HACEK organisms
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone is inadequate for prosthetic valve endocarditis and misses staphylococcal organisms. It is appropriate for native valve streptococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin combination for streptococcal native valve endocarditis cover
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin and gentamicin alone cover oral streptococci but miss staphylococcal prosthetic valve endocarditis, which is the dominant pathogen group within 12 months of surgery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin monotherapy at high dose for community-acquired Staphylococcus aureus cover
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy fails to cover methicillin-resistant staphylococci, coagulase-negative staphylococci, and biofilm-associated organisms typical of early prosthetic valve infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin, gentamicin, and rifampicin combination for early prosthetic valve infective endocarditis cover
options.rationale: Prosthetic valve endocarditis within 12 months of surgery is treated empirically with vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin to cover methicillin-resistant staphylococci including coagulase-negative species, which dominate this window. Rifampicin penetrates biofilm on prosthetic material. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Defer empirical antibiotics until echocardiographic vegetation is demonstrated to avoid unnecessary therapy
options.rationale: Once endocarditis is clinically suspected with new murmur and persistent fever after sending three culture sets, empirical antibiotics are commenced immediately because delay increases embolic and mortality risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

explanation.summary: Infective endocarditis is classified by valve type and timing. Early prosthetic valve endocarditis (less than 12 months from surgery) is dominated by Staphylococcus aureus, coagulase-negative staphylococci, and enterococci, with high methicillin resistance. Empirical therapy is vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin, narrowed when cultures return. Modified Duke criteria require 2 major, 1 major plus 3 minor, or 5 minor for definite IE. Three blood culture sets are drawn from separate sites before antibiotics. Transoesophageal echo has higher sensitivity than transthoracic for prosthetic valve vegetations.
key_points: Early PVE (under 12 months): vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampicin empirical
Late PVE (over 12 months): treated similarly to native valve disease
Three blood culture sets from separate sites before antibiotics
TOE > TTE sensitivity for prosthetic valve vegetations
explanation.pearls: Rifampicin is added in prosthetic valve endocarditis specifically because of its biofilm penetration; it is not used in native valve disease and has potent CYP3A4 induction with major drug-interaction implications.

sources.label: ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis
Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand - Endocarditis

stem:
You are the medical registrar at a metropolitan emergency department. A 54-year-old man presents with severe headache, blurred vision, and a few hours of dyspnoea. He has poorly controlled essential hypertension on irregular amlodipine. He has no chest pain. He has been working long hours and has not taken his medication for 1 week. Fundoscopy reveals papilloedema and flame haemorrhages.

CXR shows pulmonary oedema. Troponin is mildly elevated and ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with strain. There is no evidence of aortic dissection on the bedside ultrasound. The consultant asks for your initial intravenous antihypertensive plan.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, BP 232/142 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 91% room air
data_table.Examination: Bibasal crackles, JVP 4 cm elevated, papilloedema, flame haemorrhages, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 0.08 micrograms/L (peak), creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2, urinalysis 2+ blood 2+ protein, CXR pulmonary oedema, ECG sinus tachycardia with LVH

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial intravenous antihypertensive strategy?

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion titrated to reduce mean arterial pressure by approximately 25% over the first hour
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema is treated with an intravenous nitrate (GTN) infusion plus loop diuretic, with target reduction in MAP of approximately 25% within the first hour and to 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours to avoid hypoperfusion. GTN reduces both preload and afterload. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines

options.text: Oral nifedipine 20 mg immediate release with target normotension within 30 minutes of administration
options.rationale: Oral short-acting nifedipine causes uncontrolled hypotension and rebound stroke or myocardial ischaemia. It is not appropriate for hypertensive emergency. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines

options.text: Intravenous esmolol bolus and infusion as monotherapy with target systolic BP under 120 mmHg in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Pure beta-blockade in pulmonary oedema worsens cardiac decompensation and the rapid normalisation target risks cerebral and coronary hypoperfusion. Beta-blockade is reserved for selected scenarios like aortic dissection alongside a vasodilator. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 10 mg bolus repeated every 20 minutes until systolic BP under 140 mmHg
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia (problematic in possible coronary ischaemia) and is unpredictable for titration. It is not first-line in hypertensive emergency outside pregnancy. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines

options.text: Oral captopril 25 mg sublingually as rapid renin-angiotensin blockade for malignant hypertension
options.rationale: Sublingual or oral captopril is unpredictable and risks precipitous hypotension and AKI in a patient with rising creatinine. ACE inhibition has no role in acute IV management of hypertensive emergency. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is severe hypertension (commonly above 180/120) with acute end-organ damage (encephalopathy, retinopathy, pulmonary oedema, acute kidney injury, myocardial ischaemia, aortic dissection). Lowering blood pressure too rapidly causes watershed cerebral and coronary infarction. Targets are approximately 25% reduction in MAP within the first hour, then to 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours, except in aortic dissection (systolic under 120 mmHg within 20 minutes) and ischaemic stroke (carefully titrated). IV nitrates, labetalol, and clevidipine are common Australian choices.
key_points: Hypertensive emergency: severe BP plus acute end-organ damage
Target: 25% MAP reduction in first hour, then 160/100 by 2 to 6 hours
Aortic dissection: systolic under 120 mmHg within 20 minutes
Pulmonary oedema: IV nitrate plus loop diuretic; avoid pure beta-blockade
explanation.pearls: Excessive rapid BP lowering in hypertensive emergency causes watershed cerebral infarction because chronic hypertension shifts the cerebral autoregulation curve - aiming for 25% MAP reduction in the first hour respects this rightward shift.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation of Australia - Hypertension Guidelines
ESC Guidelines for the Management of Arterial Hypertension

stem:
You are the medical registrar called urgently to the ward for a 72-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease stage 4 admitted yesterday with a vomiting illness. Her usual medications include ramipril 10 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, and metoprolol. Today she is alert but feeling weak. The phlebotomist reports the morning bloods have just returned and the lab has phoned a critical potassium result.

The intern has performed a 12-lead ECG which shows peaked T waves throughout the precordial leads and a widening QRS. Intravenous access is patent. She is not currently dialysed and has no permanent access.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 58 bpm, BP 134/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air, Temp 36.6 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Alert, no peripheral oedema, no tremor, normal heart sounds, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 7.4 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 348 micromol/L, bicarbonate 16 mmol/L, glucose 6.4 mmol/L, ECG peaked T waves with QRS 130 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first intervention?

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g as the initial intervention to bind gut potassium
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins have a slow onset (hours) and are inappropriate as initial therapy for cardiotoxic hyperkalaemia. They are an adjunct for ongoing removal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Intravenous insulin actrapid 10 units in dextrose 50% 50 mL with the goal of intracellular potassium shift
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose lowers serum potassium but does nothing to stabilise the cardiac membrane in the 5 to 15 minute window before its onset. Calcium must come first when ECG changes are present. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Salbutamol 10 to 20 mg nebulised as initial monotherapy to shift potassium into cells
options.rationale: Salbutamol provides modest potassium-shifting effect but does not stabilise the myocardium. It is an adjunct to calcium, insulin-dextrose, and definitive removal, not a first step in cardiotoxic hyperkalaemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: When ECG changes accompany hyperkalaemia, intravenous calcium gluconate is given immediately to stabilise the cardiac membrane before any potassium-lowering measure. Onset is within minutes and effect lasts 30 to 60 minutes, providing time for definitive treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis as the first intervention while preparations are made for vascular access
options.rationale: Dialysis is the definitive removal step but takes time to organise, and an unprotected myocardium meanwhile can arrest. Calcium gluconate is the immediate priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia management proceeds in three phases: stabilise the myocardium, shift potassium intracellularly, and remove total body potassium. Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 2 to 3 minutes is the membrane stabiliser with onset within minutes. Shifting therapies include insulin-dextrose (10 units actrapid with 50 mL 50% dextrose) and nebulised salbutamol. Bicarbonate is added if metabolic acidosis is present. Definitive removal uses loop diuretics, cation exchange (sodium zirconium cyclosilicate or sodium polystyrene sulfonate), or dialysis. Withhold any contributing medications such as ACE inhibitors and aldosterone antagonists.
key_points: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV first when ECG changes present
Insulin-dextrose and salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly within 15 to 30 minutes
Loop diuretics or cation exchange resins for ongoing removal
Dialysis is definitive for refractory or severe hyperkalaemia
explanation.pearls: Calcium gluconate works within minutes but only lasts 30 to 60 minutes - it buys time but does not remove potassium, so insulin-dextrose, dialysis preparation, and cessation of contributing drugs must follow immediately.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5 - Medications in Cardiac Arrest

stem: You are the medical registrar called to the high dependency unit. A 48-year-old woman was admitted yesterday for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. During induction of anaesthesia 30 minutes ago she developed extreme hypertension and tachycardia followed by an episode of hypotension. She has now been transferred to the high dependency unit. She had been investigated 6 months ago for episodic headache, sweating, and palpitations but did not attend her endocrine appointment. The surgical team and the anaesthetist suspect an undiagnosed endocrine tumour. Plasma fractionated metanephrines have been sent. She is currently haemodynamically labile.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138 bpm (sinus), BP currently 192/118 mmHg fluctuating, RR 22, Temp 37.1 degrees C, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, no focal neurology, no goitre, no abdominal mass palpable, no signs of NF1 stigmata
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, troponin 0.04 micrograms/L, glucose 11 mmol/L, plasma free metanephrines pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate antihypertensive strategy?

options.text: Commence intravenous phentolamine and fluid resuscitation, with a beta-blocker added only after alpha-blockade
options.rationale: Unopposed beta-blockade in suspected phaeochromocytoma crisis precipitates catastrophic alpha-mediated hypertension because circulating catecholamines lose their beta-vasodilatory counter. Phentolamine (non-selective alpha-blocker) is first-line for the acute crisis; sodium nitroprusside is an alternative. Beta-blockade is added only after alpha-blockade is established to control reflex tachycardia. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma

options.text: Commence intravenous metoprolol bolus to control the heart rate and reduce myocardial oxygen demand
options.rationale: Unopposed beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma crisis is contraindicated because removal of beta-mediated vasodilation unmasks unopposed alpha-vasoconstriction, causing severe hypertensive crisis and possible cardiogenic shock. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma

options.text: Commence intravenous labetalol infusion as combined alpha and beta blockade for catecholamine crisis
options.rationale: Labetalol has a 7:1 beta-to-alpha ratio when given intravenously and is functionally a beta-predominant agent in phaeochromocytoma, risking the same paradoxical hypertension as pure beta-blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma

options.text: Commence intravenous amiodarone 300 mg bolus over 20 minutes to suppress sinus tachycardia
options.rationale: The tachycardia is catecholamine-driven and amiodarone is not the appropriate agent. Treatment must address the catecholamine excess via alpha-blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 5 g loading dose with cessation of the surgical procedure
options.rationale: Magnesium is an adjunct but not a primary agent for phaeochromocytoma crisis. Phentolamine or nitroprusside provides specific alpha-mediated vasodilation matched to the pathophysiology. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma crisis arises from massive catecholamine release, often during anaesthesia, surgical manipulation, or interventional procedures in an undiagnosed or inadequately blocked patient. The cardinal management rule is alpha-blockade first, then beta-blockade for tachycardia. Acute IV options include phentolamine (non-selective alpha), sodium nitroprusside, and nicardipine. Volume resuscitation addresses contracted intravascular volume. Once stabilised, oral phenoxybenzamine is used for 10 to 14 days of preoperative blockade before definitive surgical removal. Plasma free metanephrines are the diagnostic test of choice.
key_points: Phaeochromocytoma crisis: alpha-blockade first, beta-blockade only after alpha is established
Acute IV options: phentolamine, nitroprusside, nicardipine
Plasma free metanephrines have the highest sensitivity for diagnosis
Preoperative oral phenoxybenzamine for 10 to 14 days plus volume loading before surgery
explanation.pearls: Unopposed beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma can precipitate fatal hypertensive crisis within minutes because removing beta-mediated vasodilation unleashes catecholamine alpha-vasoconstriction - the order of blockade is non-negotiable.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline - Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma
